blurb: Tall, airy wands of brilliant scarlet tubular flowers from early summer into fall — a hummingbird favorite that shrugs off heat and drought on the lean Front Range.
size: ~2–3 ft tall × 1–2 ft wide (flower stalks to 4 ft)
seasons: Scarlet tubular flowers early summer–fall; narrow blue-green foliage; tan seed stalks in winter
wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; also native bees

care.hardiness: Cold-hardy across the Front Range — USDA zones 4–8 (Boulder is ~5b–6a), a Southwestern-mountain native perennial that overwinters reliably here in well-drained ground.[2]
care.planting: Plant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into lean unamended soil; give it the hottest, best-drained spot you have. In containers use a gritty fast-draining mix and avoid wet feet — potted crowns rot far more easily than plants in open ground.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — six-plus hours — for upright, well-colored scarlet spikes; it gets leggy and shy-flowering in shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Wants sharp drainage and lean soil; thrives in average, dry, even shallow rocky ground and resents Boulder's heavy clay. Do not amend with compost — rich, wet soil causes root rot.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate, xeric once established; needs spring moisture to bloom but overwatering (especially after flowering, or in poorly drained soil) brings on root and crown rot.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 12–18 in apart; clumps reach roughly 2–3 ft tall and 1–1.5 ft wide.[2]
care.propagation: Grow from seed, which germinates best after cold-moist stratification (fall-sow outdoors, or chill moist in the fridge before a late-winter start). Penstemons resent division — don't split the crown; take basal/softwood cuttings to clone a plant instead.[3][1]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed (fall sowing supplies natural winter chilling) to break dormancy and germinate well.[3]
care.bloom: Tall wands of brilliant scarlet, tubular hummingbird flowers from late spring into summer (about May–June here, sometimes longer); deadhead to neaten and prolong the plant.[1][2][3]
care.feeding: No fertilizer needed in normal garden soil — feeding produces floppy growth and shortens its life.[1]
care.maintenance: Low: deadhead spent spikes, then back off summer water to prevent crown rot; tidy old flower stems in late fall or early spring.[1]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases; root rot strikes in wet, poorly drained or over-amended soil, and grasshoppers/flea beetles/rust are occasional.[1][2]
care.companions: A hummingbird magnet for hot, dry borders and rock gardens — combine with other xeric natives and grasses in lean sunny ground.[2]

references.name: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Penstemon barbatus
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Penstemon barbatus

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its hummingbird flowers — no documented edible use.[3]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[3]
